title: Reading and Use of English - Part 5
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

content.title: The Lazarus Taxon: Beyond Extinction
content.subtitle: How 'living fossils' and ancient DNA are challenging the finality of biological loss.
content.text: Extinction has long been considered the ultimate 'stop' in the narrative of life—a permanent erasure from the fossil record. However, the discovery of 'Lazarus taxa' (species that disappear from the fossil record only to reappear millions of years later) has complicated this view. [31] These discoveries suggest that our understanding of extinction is often a reflection of the gaps in our data rather than a true biological consensus. [/31] A species might not be dead; it might simply be hiding in a region that does not favor fossilization. || The most famous example is the Coelacanth, a prehistoric fish thought to have died out 66 million years ago until one was pulled from a fishing net in 1938. [32] This 'living fossil' provides a direct link to the past, offering insights into the transition from sea-dwelling to land-dwelling vertebrates that a crushed bone never could. [/32] It is a reminder that the deep ocean remains one of the last great archives of the planet's history, a place where time seems to have slowed down. || [33] Today, the conversation has shifted from finding survivors to 'de-extinction'—the controversial attempt to bring species back using genetic engineering. [/33] Using fragments of DNA recovered from museum specimens or frozen tundra, scientists are attempting to 'edit' the genome of living relatives to recreate something resembling a Woolly Mammoth or a Passenger Pigeon. Proponents argue that this could restore ecological balance and undo some of the damage caused by human activity. || [34] However, critics argue that these 'resurrected' animals would be mere shadows of their ancestors. [/34] An animal is more than its DNA; it is its behavior, its diet, and its relationship to an ecosystem that may no longer exist. A mammoth born to an elephant surrogate in a 21st-century zoo would have no one to teach it how to be a mammoth. It would be a biological curiosity, isolated in a world that has moved on. || [35] Furthermore, there is the 'moral hazard' of de-extinction. If we believe we can simply bring species back later, the urgency to protect endangered species today may be severely diminished. [/35] Conservation resources are finite; every dollar spent on a high-tech lab is a dollar not spent on protecting a currently living habitat. The 'techno-fix' becomes a distraction from the messy, difficult work of traditional conservation. || [36] Ultimately, the study of extinction reminds us of the delicate 'interconnectedness' of life. [/36] Whether through the discovery of a Coelacanth or the birth of a gene-edited hybrid, we are forced to confront the fact that we are part of a long, fragile chain. The goal should perhaps not be to play God with the past, but to become better stewards of the present. Extinction is a natural process, but the current rate of loss is anything but natural, and no amount of technology can replace a lost world.

questions.question: What does the author suggest about 'Lazarus taxa' in the first paragraph?
options: A) They prove that extinction is a reversible process in nature.
B) They indicate that the fossil record is an incomplete source of information.
C) They are species that have been successfully brought back by scientists.
D) They show that most extinctions are actually caused by lack of data.
questions.explanation: The author states that these discoveries suggest our understanding of extinction reflects 'gaps in our data' rather than actual biological facts.

questions.question: Why is the Coelacanth considered particularly valuable to science?
options: A) It is the oldest fish species to ever be discovered.
B) It allows researchers to study a living example of an evolutionary transition.
C) It proves that the deep ocean is full of prehistoric monsters.
D) It is the only fish that can survive both in water and on land.
questions.explanation: The text says it provides a 'direct link to the past' regarding the transition from sea to land, offering insights that fossils cannot.

questions.question: The goal of 'de-extinction' is to...
options: A) find more living fossils in the deep ocean.
B) protect endangered species from going extinct.
C) use biotechnology to recreate extinct animals.
D) prove that genetic engineering is a safe practice.
questions.explanation: The text describes it as an 'attempt to bring species back using genetic engineering' by editing the genome of living relatives.

questions.question: What is the main concern of the critics regarding resurrected species?
options: A) They would be too dangerous to keep in modern zoos.
B) They would lack the learned behaviors and environment of their predecessors.
C) They would be genetically identical to living elephants.
D) They would be unable to reproduce in the 21st century.
questions.explanation: Critics argue that an animal is more than DNA; it needs its 'behavior, its diet, and its relationship to an ecosystem' to truly be its ancestor.

questions.question: What does the 'moral hazard' refer to in the fifth paragraph?
options: A) The ethical problems of editing the genes of living animals.
B) The possibility that people will stop caring about current conservation efforts.
C) The risk of high-tech labs causing new types of extinctions.
D) The high cost of technology compared to the low success rate of de-extinction.
questions.explanation: The text defines this hazard as the risk that the 'urgency to protect endangered species today may be severely diminished' if we think we can bring them back later.

questions.question: What is the author's final conclusion about extinction?
options: A) It is a natural process that humans should not try to influence.
B) Technology will eventually be able to replace all lost worlds.
C) We should focus on preserving existing life rather than resurrecting the past.
D) The current rate of species loss is part of a natural cycle.
questions.explanation: The author concludes that we should 'become better stewards of the present' and that technology cannot replace a lost world.
